tips: CRITICAL: chicken jerky MUST be brought to 74°C internal before or during drying. This is a food safety requirement, not a suggestion. Pre-heat in an oven at 165°C for 10 min if your dehydrator can't reach this temperature.
Use breast meat for cleaner jerky (no fat), or thighs for more flavour at the cost of shorter shelf life (fat goes rancid faster).
Refrigerate chicken jerky — it doesn't have the same shelf stability as beef or venison jerky. Consume within 1 week or freeze.

cuts.name: Salmon Jerky
rubs: Sweet soy cure: soy sauce, brown sugar, garlic, ginger, black pepper. Marinate skin-off fillets 4-8 hrs before drying.
Maple and pepper: maple syrup, cracked black pepper, soy sauce, garlic powder. Apply as a glaze before and during drying.
Lemon and dill cure: lemon juice, dill, salt, brown sugar, cracked pepper. Classic Scandinavian approach.
woods: Alder — traditional wood for smoked salmon. If hot-smoking before dehydrating, alder is the choice.
Apple — lighter alternative, broadly available.
Cherry — slightly richer than apple. Good option.
tips: Salmon jerky is actually smoked and then dried (or dried fully). The fat content means it won't have the long shelf life of lean-meat jerky — refrigerate and consume within 3 days.
Remove pin bones before slicing. Run your fingers along the fillet to find them, then pull out with tweezers or pliers.
Slice on an angle against the grain into strips 0.5-1 cm thick. The oil in salmon helps it stay more pliable than lean meat jerky.

cuts.name: Pork Jerky
rubs: Honey soy: soy sauce, honey, garlic, ginger, five spice, sesame oil. Asian-influenced and outstanding.
BBQ classic: soy sauce, Worcestershire, smoked paprika, garlic powder, brown sugar, black pepper, cayenne.
Teriyaki: soy, mirin, sake, sugar, garlic, ginger. Can't go wrong.
woods: Apple — naturally pairs with pork jerky. Sweet and mild.
Cherry — slightly richer, beautiful colour.
Hickory — for bold BBQ-flavoured pork jerky. Use sparingly.
tips: Use loin or tenderloin for the leanest pork jerky. Like chicken, fat in pork jerky reduces shelf life significantly.
Pork jerky must reach 71°C internal at some point during the process. This is a food safety requirement for pork.
Slice uniformly at 3-5mm thick — use a deli slicer if you have access to one, or partially freeze the pork for easier hand-slicing.

cuts.name: Venison Leg
rubs: Herb and juniper: juniper berries (crushed), garlic, rosemary, thyme, coarse salt, cracked black pepper, olive oil. Traditional game herb combination.
Red wine marinade: red wine, garlic, rosemary, thyme, juniper, bay leaf, olive oil, salt, pepper — 12-24 hr marinade that also tenderises.
SPG with paprika: coarse salt, black pepper, garlic powder, smoked paprika. Simple and effective — lets the venison flavour shine.
woods: Cherry — mild fruity smoke is the classic choice for venison. Doesn't fight with the gamey flavour.
Oak — clean and honest, good backbone for a long leg cook.
Apple — lighter than cherry, very clean. Good for those who find venison strong.
tips: Venison is very lean — it dries out faster than beef or lamb. Don't push past 71°C for the leg; it's best at 63-65°C for slicing (still pink).
A fat cover or barding (wrapping in bacon or fat) during the cook helps keep the lean meat moist.
The gamey flavour intensifies if the meat is stressed during slaughter or handled poorly. Good quality, properly hung venison shouldn't need aggressive de-gaming.

cuts.name: Venison Shoulder (Pulled)
rubs: SPG plus: coarse salt, black pepper, garlic powder, smoked paprika. Apply 12+ hrs ahead for the long cook.
Wild spice: juniper, black pepper, garlic, dried thyme, caraway, salt. A gamey cut needs a bold rub.
Coffee and cocoa: espresso, cocoa, garlic powder, smoked paprika, coarse salt, black pepper. Rich and complex — excellent on venison shoulder.
woods: Oak — the best for a long pulled venison cook. Clean and reliable.
Cherry — beautiful colour and mild fruitiness. A great pairing.
Hickory — only if you like bold smoke AND bold game flavour together. Not for everyone.
tips: Venison shoulder has little fat — moisture is the challenge. Wrap in butcher paper earlier than pork shoulder (at 65°C) to protect from drying out.
Add a butter and beef stock baste when wrapping to compensate for the lack of natural fat.
For pulling, cook to 90-93°C. The collagen in shoulder is just enough to give the pulled meat texture without turning greasy.

cuts.name: Kangaroo Rump Steak
rubs: Bush spice: native pepperberry (or black pepper), lemon myrtle, garlic powder, salt, olive oil. Use Australian native spices for authenticity.
Coffee rub: espresso powder, garlic powder, smoked paprika, salt, cracked black pepper. Kangaroo is lean and gamey — strong flavours work.
Simple salt and pepper: coarse salt, cracked black pepper. Pull at 55°C rare. The lean meat needs nothing more.
woods: Wattleseed wood (or similar Australian native hardwood) — if available, unbeatable pairing for kangaroo.
Cherry — mild fruity smoke, very appropriate for lean game meat.
Apple — light and clean. Preserves the unique kangaroo flavour.
tips: Kangaroo is extremely lean — the leanest of all game meats. Cook to medium-rare (55°C) MAXIMUM or it becomes tough and dry.
Marinate in olive oil, garlic, and herbs for 2-4 hrs to help with moisture retention during cooking.
Rest for 10-15 min after cooking. Slice thinly across the grain. Serve immediately.

cuts.name: Wild Boar Shoulder
rubs: Tuscan herbs: rosemary, fennel seeds (crushed), garlic, sage, salt, cracked pepper, olive oil. Classic Italian approach to wild boar.
Spicy paprika: smoked paprika, garlic powder, cumin, cayenne, brown sugar, salt, cracked pepper. BBQ-style rub for a long smoke.
Wine and herb: red wine reduction, garlic, thyme, rosemary, salt, black pepper as a marinade/rub. Softens the game flavour.
woods: Oak — best for wild boar. Medium smoke that holds up to the rich, gamey meat.
Hickory — bold and assertive. Works if you embrace the game flavour.
Cherry — softer option with beautiful colour. Good for those who want milder smoke.
tips: Wild boar is gamier and leaner than domestic pork. Marinating for 12-24 hrs is highly recommended to tenderise and moderate the game flavour.
Cook like a pork shoulder to 90-95°C for pulling. The collagen content varies but generally it needs the full treatment.
Serve with bold accompaniments — polenta, gremolata, mustard, pickled vegetables. Wild boar can handle strong flavours.

cuts.name: Duck Legs (Smoked)
rubs: Five spice and soy: five spice, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, sesame oil, brown sugar. Chinese BBQ-inspired. Outstanding.
Duck confit seasoning: coarse salt, black pepper, fresh thyme, garlic, bay leaf — cure overnight, then cook slow.
Simple herb: garlic, rosemary, thyme, olive oil, salt, cracked pepper. Let the duck flavour come through.
woods: Cherry — the definitive wood for duck legs. Fruity and mild.
Apple — lighter cherry alternative. Beautiful pairing.
Orange wood or orange peel on coals — traditional and incredible with duck.
tips: Score the skin in a crosshatch pattern to help the fat render. Duck legs have a lot of subcutaneous fat — this needs to go during cooking.
The fat that renders off is excellent duck fat — catch it in a drip tray and save it for roasting potatoes.
Duck legs are forgiving — push to 82-85°C for fully rendered fat and very tender meat. They don't dry out like breast meat.

cuts.name: Rabbit (Whole)
rubs: Herb and mustard: Dijon mustard as a binder, then fresh thyme, rosemary, garlic, salt, cracked pepper. Classic French approach.
Mediterranean: olive oil, lemon zest, garlic, dried oregano, fresh parsley, salt, cracked pepper. Simple and fresh.
Paprika and garlic: smoked paprika, garlic powder, olive oil, salt, cracked black pepper, pinch of cayenne. Spanish-style.
woods: Apple — mild and sweet, ideal for delicate rabbit.
Cherry — slight fruitiness, works beautifully.
Pecan — slightly nutty, good match for rabbit.
tips: Rabbit is lean and dries out quickly. Basting every 30 min with butter or oil is important. Consider barding (wrapping) with bacon strips.
The saddle (back loin) cooks faster than the legs and shoulders. If cooking whole, shield the saddle with foil if it's getting ahead of the rest.
Rabbit loves a braising finish: smoke for 1-2 hrs for colour and smoke, then braise in wine and stock until fall-apart tender.

cuts.name: Pheasant (Whole)
rubs: Herb butter under skin: softened butter, garlic, thyme, sage, salt, pepper pushed under the breast skin. Essential for a lean bird.
Bacon wrap: no rub needed — wrap the entire breast in overlapping bacon rashers before cooking. This bastes the lean meat from the outside.
Simple herb oil: olive oil, garlic, thyme, rosemary, salt, pepper applied all over.
woods: Apple — gentle smoke for a delicate game bird.
Cherry — mild fruitiness, good balance.
Pecan — slightly richer option, still mild enough for pheasant.
tips: Pheasant is extremely lean — it dries out faster than chicken. Barding with bacon or using butter under the skin is not optional for a moist result.
Pull at 74°C internal in the thigh. Rest 15 min. The breast will be very well done at this point — that's the nature of a lean game bird.
Best results come from a slow cook method (slow cooker or covered low oven) with liquid. Pure smoking a whole pheasant requires constant basting.

cuts.name: Emu Steak
rubs: Bush pepper and salt: native pepperberry (or black pepper), sea salt, garlic powder, olive oil. Let the unique emu flavour speak.
Red wine marinade: red wine, garlic, rosemary, olive oil, salt, black pepper — 4-8 hrs. Tenderises this lean, muscular meat.
Simple coffee rub: espresso powder, garlic powder, smoked paprika, coarse salt, cracked black pepper. Bold flavours for a bold meat.
woods: Cherry — mild fruitiness is the best match for emu.
Apple — clean and light, preserves the unique flavour.
Wattleseed wood (if available) — the ultimate Australian native pairing.
tips: Emu is very lean, dark, and strong-flavoured. Cook to medium-rare (55-60°C) ONLY — anything beyond medium makes it tough and gamey.
Marinate for 4-8 hrs minimum — the dense muscle fibres benefit enormously from a marinade.
Slice very thinly across the grain for the best texture. Emu steak is best served as a thin carpaccio-style slice rather than a thick cut.
